summary: English Renaissance playwright and poet. Known for Volpone, The Alchemist, Bartholomew Fair, and Epicoene. Wrote the poems 'To Celia' ('Drink to me only with thine eyes'), 'On My First Son,' and 'To Penshurst.' Eulogized Shakespeare as 'not of an age, but for all time.'

works.name: The Alchemist
works.description: Set in Lovewit's house during a plague. The butler Jeremy (disguised as 'Captain Face'), Subtle, and the prostitute Dol Common run scams. Sir Epicure Mammon desires the philosopher's stone. Dapper is blindfolded and meets the 'Queen of Fairy' (Dol Common in disguise). The angry Boy Kastril tries to get his sister to marry a Spanish count. Dutch Anabaptists led by Ananias unknowingly buy goods from Mammon. Subtle flees at the end.

clues.clue: Epicure Mammon seeks the philosopher's stone; Subtle and Face run scams
examples: Epicure Mammon is convinced that Jeremy and Subtle have the philosopher's stone

clues.clue: Dapper meets the 'Queen of Fairy' — Dol Common in disguise
examples: Dapper is blindfolded and robbed before meeting the 'Queen of Fairy'

clues.clue: Set in Lovewit's house during plague; Subtle and Dol Common flee
examples: Lovewit leaves to avoid a plague

clues.clue: Ananias and Dutch Anabaptists
examples: Dutch Anabaptists led by Ananias

works.name: Volpone
works.description: Set in Venice. The cunning Volpone feigns illness to receive gifts from legacy hunters. His servant Mosca assists the deception. Characters with animal names include Corvino (crow), Voltore (vulture), and Corbaccio (raven). Sir Politic Would-be has a get-rich-quick scheme; Peregrine humiliates him by making him hide in a wine butt pretending to be a tortoise. Volpone attempts to rape Corvino's wife Celia. Mosca is forced to become a galley slave. Bonario inherits Corbaccio's property.

clues.clue: Volpone feigns illness; servant Mosca assists; legacy hunters with animal names
examples: the servant Mosca assists the title cunning Venetian gentleman

clues.clue: Peregrine makes Politic hide as a tortoise in a wine butt
examples: Politic is duped into acting like this animal while hiding in a wine butt

clues.clue: Volpone attempts to rape Celia
examples: the disguised title character attempts to rape Corvino's wife Celia

clues.clue: Mosca becomes a galley slave; Bonario inherits
examples: a villainous servant is forced to become a galley slave

works.name: Bartholomew Fair
works.description: Features Justice Overdo in disguise, the Puritan Zeal-of-the-Land Busy, and puppets that refute accusations of cross-dressing by lifting their clothes. Tom Quarlous impersonates a man and steals a marriage license. Winwife tries to win Dame Purecraft. The play opens with a legal contract asking audience criticism to match seat price.

clues.clue: Puppets lift clothes to refute cross-dressing accusations by Zeal-of-the-Land Busy
examples: puppets lift up their clothes to reveal they have no genitals

clues.clue: Justice Overdo in disguise; Puritan characters mocked
examples: an undercover Adam Overdo discovers his wife being pimped

clues.clue: Opens with a legal contract — criticism proportional to seat price
examples: offering a legal contract requesting that the audience's criticism be proportional to their seat price

works.name: Poetry
works.description: 'To Celia' ('Song: To Celia') opens 'Drink to me only with thine eyes' and includes the image of a rosy wreath sent to the beloved that now smells of her. 'On My First Son' mourns a child who died at seven, called 'his best piece of poetry' and 'thou child of my right hand.' 'To Penshurst' is one of the earliest country house poems, praising Philip Sidney's estate as 'not built to envious show.' Eulogized Shakespeare in the First Folio with 'he was not of an age, but for all time!'

clues.clue: 'Drink to me only with thine eyes' — 'To Celia'
examples: 'Drink to me only with thine eyes'

clues.clue: Rosy wreath sent to lover — now 'smells not of itself, but thee'
examples: the 'rosy wreath' he got back from his lover smells 'Not of itself, but thee'

clues.clue: 'On My First Son' — child called 'his best piece of poetry'
examples: his 'best piece of poetry'

clues.clue: 'To Penshurst' — earliest country house poem; 'not built to envious show'
examples: 'not built to envious show'

clues.clue: 'He was not of an age, but for all time!' — Shakespeare eulogy in First Folio
examples: 'he was not of an age, but for all time!'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: English Renaissance playwright. Former bricklayer who killed Gabriel Spenser in a duel. Feuded with John Marston and Thomas Dekker in the 'War of the Theatres' (parodied Marston in Cynthia's Revels; Dekker parodied Jonson as Horace in Satiromastix). Hawthornden Manuscripts record his conversations with William Drummond. Wrote The Isle of Dogs with Thomas Nashe (banned). Presided over the Apollo Club; the Cavalier poets called themselves the 'Tribe of Ben' or 'Sons of Ben.' Also wrote Epicoene (Dauphine's uncle Morose marries a man disguised as a 'Silent Woman'), Every Man in His Humour (Dickens revived it), the Masque of Blackness, and 'A Fit of Rhyme against Rhyme.' Popularized comedy of humours.

clues.clue: 'War of the Theatres' with Marston and Dekker
examples: feuded with John Marston and Thomas Dekker in the 'War of the Theatres'

clues.clue: Killed Gabriel Spenser in a duel; former bricklayer
examples: killed Gabriel Spenser in a duel

clues.clue: Cavalier poets were 'Tribe/Sons of Ben'
examples: the Cavalier poets self-identified as the 'Sons of' this author

clues.clue: Hawthornden Manuscripts — conversations with William Drummond
examples: conversations with William Drummond published as the Hawthornden Manuscripts

clues.clue: Wrote masques — Masque of Blackness for Anne of Denmark
examples: The Masque of Blackness

clues.clue: Popularized comedy of humours — characters with exaggerated obsessions
examples: popularized a genre in which characters have exaggerated obsessions

comprehensive_summary:
Ben Jonson (1572-1637) was an English Renaissance playwright and poet, a towering figure in Jacobean drama. He is known for his satirical comedies and his influential poetry.

The Alchemist is set in Lovewit's house during a plague, where the butler Jeremy (disguised as 'Captain Face'), Subtle, and the prostitute Dol Common run scams. Sir Epicure Mammon seeks the philosopher's stone, and Dapper meets the 'Queen of Fairy' (Dol Common in disguise). Volpone is set in Venice where the cunning title character feigns illness to receive gifts from legacy hunters like Corvino, Voltore, and Corbaccio, assisted by his servant Mosca. Bartholomew Fair features puppets refuting cross-dressing accusations by lifting their clothes, and mocks Puritans like Zeal-of-the-Land Busy.

Jonson's poetry includes 'To Celia' ('Drink to me only with thine eyes'), 'On My First Son' (mourning a child called 'his best piece of poetry'), and 'To Penshurst' (an early country house poem). He eulogized Shakespeare in the First Folio with 'he was not of an age, but for all time!'

Jonson was a former bricklayer who killed Gabriel Spenser in a duel. He feuded with Marston and Dekker in the 'War of the Theatres.' The Hawthornden Manuscripts record his conversations with William Drummond. The Cavalier poets called themselves his 'Tribe' or 'Sons.' He also wrote Epicoene, Every Man in His Humour, and masques like the Masque of Blackness.
